one_line_takeaway: First study to port systems-security TOCTOU to LLM agents: introduces TOCTOU-Bench (66 tasks, 56 vulnerable) and three stage-specific defenses whose combined effect is real but modest -- vulnerable trajectories 12%->8% and a 95% attack-window reduction, with automated detection still weak at 25% TPR.
key_finding: Each defense provides only partial protection and the study reads as an honest negative-leaning first cut. Prompt Rewriting reduced vulnerable plans marginally (55 -> 53 tasks) but crucially introduced no new vulnerabilities (no originally-safe task became vulnerable). SIM's automated detection is weak -- 25% TPR / 0.62 AUC on ground-truth sequences, dropping to 14% TPR / 0.57 AUC on planner-generated trajectories -- because it relies only on tool descriptions and lacks the query context and plan information the manual labeling used. Tool Fuser is the strongest component: it shrinks the attack window between vulnerable calls from ~1.7s to ~0.07s via atomic fused execution. Combining all three reduces executed trajectories containing vulnerabilities from 12% to 8% and cuts the attack window by ~95% (1.77s -> 0.08s).
core_contribution: The paper presents the first systematic study of Time-of-Check to Time-of-Use (TOCTOU) vulnerabilities in LLM-enabled agents, a class of race-condition flaws where an agent validates external state (a file, an inbox, an API response) in one tool call and acts on it in a later call, during which an adversary or external process can modify that state. The authors make three concrete contributions of their own: (1) TOCTOU-Bench, a benchmark of 66 realistic user tasks (56 hand-labeled as containing a possible TOCTOU vulnerability) derived from AgentDojo; (2) three defenses each targeting a distinct stage of the agent workflow -- Prompt Rewriting (prompt stage), State Integrity Monitoring / SIM (planning stage), and Tool Fuser (tool-calling stage); and (3) an empirical evaluation of these defenses on the benchmark. The framing is explicitly a bridge between systems-security TOCTOU literature and AI agent safety.
threat_model: The adversary (or an uncoordinated external process) can modify shared environment state (e.g., an email, a calendar entry, a Slack channel, a cloud-drive file, a database record) during the temporal gap that exists between two successive, non-atomic tool calls made by an LLM agent. Because agent tool executions are interleaved with natural-language reasoning and are not atomic, the agent's later 'use' action operates on state different from what it 'checked'. Consequences the authors cite include malicious configuration swaps, payload injection, unsafe tool use, data leakage, and bypass of safety rules. Notably the authors explicitly scope OUT prompt injection (they remove AgentDojo injection tasks), distinguishing TOCTOU from the prompt-based and data-exfiltration threats prior work covers.
limitations: Effectiveness is modest across the board: 25% peak detection TPR, only a 2-task (55->53) reduction in vulnerable plans, and residual 8% vulnerable trajectories after combining all defenses. The authors themselves attribute the small prompt-rewriting gain to the explicit, tightly-constrained AgentDojo task prompts (open-ended tasks would show more benefit) and attribute SIM's weakness to using tool descriptions alone without query/plan context. Scope limits: a single LLM (GPT-4o), a single underlying benchmark (AgentDojo), only 66 tasks, Tool Fuser timing evaluated only in the Slack environment, and 'possible/potential' vulnerability labels are hand-assigned rather than demonstrated via a concrete executed exploit. It is a preprint (6 pages of body) and is exploratory rather than a mature defense. No comparison against any external competing defense (none exists yet for this specific problem).

datasets_used: TOCTOU-Bench (authors' own benchmark: 66 user tasks, 56 labeled possibly vulnerable, built on AgentDojo)
AgentDojo (source framework; 97 original tasks filtered to 66; Banking, Slack, Travel, Workspace environments)
quantitative_results: 66 tasks in TOCTOU-Bench (filtered from AgentDojo's 97); 56 of 66 hand-labeled as possibly vulnerable
Prompt Rewriting: vulnerable plans reduced from 55 to 53 tasks; 0 originally-safe tasks became vulnerable
SIM detection on ground-truth sequences (Baseline): 25% TPR, 0.62 AUC
SIM detection on planner-generated trajectories (Ours): 14% TPR, 0.57 AUC
Tool Fuser (Slack env): attack window between vulnerable tool calls 1.70+/-0.90s -> 0.07+/-0.01s
Combined all three defenses: executed trajectories containing vulnerabilities 12% -> 8%
baselines_compared: SIM on AgentDojo ground-truth tool sequences (labeled 'Baseline') vs. SIM on planner-generated trajectories (labeled 'Ours') -- an internal comparison, not an external competing method
Original vs. rewritten prompts for the Prompt Rewriting evaluation
Tool calls with vs. without fusion for the Tool Fuser timing evaluation
No external/prior defense baseline exists; the authors state this is the first work to study TOCTOU in LLM agents
